one_line_takeaway: DeepClean forgets designated training data by zeroing and re-tuning only the weights whose Fisher-diagonal ratio favors the forget set, matching gold-model privacy (MIA) behavior on MNIST/Cifar at roughly 3-60x less compute than Fisher/Hessian baselines - but with manual thresholds, MIA-only privacy evidence, and only small image CNNs tested.
key_finding: On Cifar-10 random-sample unlearning (Table 1), DeepClean reaches near-gold behavior far faster than the baselines: VGG-16 AccDr 95.62% with Delta-AccDf -3.88% and Delta-MIA -2.84% in 60s; ResNet-18 AccDr 98.74%, Delta-AccDf -3.68%, Delta-MIA -5.33% in 71s. The gold model itself needed 201s (VGG-16) / 284s (ResNet-18). Competing methods are markedly worse on the speed/quality trade-off: Fisher Forgetting collapses the retain accuracy to ~10% and costs 3260-3743s; L-CODEC updates too few weights (Delta-AccDf up to +100% on label unlearning, Delta-MIA +65-67%) and costs 245-479s; Sparse MU matches forgetting but with lower utility (AccDr ~88-92%) at 133-153s; Teacher is highly unstable across datasets (e.g. AccDr drops to 34-69%). For whole-class 'Label' unlearning DeepClean achieves Delta-AccDf +0.00% (full class removal) with Delta-MIA between -8.04% and +3.64%. Ablation Table 4 confirms that simply zeroing the selected weights (Zero Weights) destroys utility (AccDr 16.5% on Cifar-10 RN), so the subsequent retrain-on-Dr step is essential.
core_contribution: DeepClean is a lightweight approximate machine-unlearning algorithm that uses only the DIAGONAL of the empirical Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) to decide which weights to forget. For each trainable weight w_i it computes the ratio r(w_i)=I_{Df}(w_i)/I_{Dr}(w_i) of the FIM-diagonal entry on the forget set Df to that on the retain set Dr; weights with r(w_i)>gamma are deemed to encode Df-specific information. These weights are then reset to zero and fine-tuned on the retain set Dr while ALL other weights are frozen. The authors' own novelty claims are: (1) using the FIM diagonal (rather than full FIM inversion or a Hessian approximation) so no large matrix inversion is needed; (2) requiring NO stored gradients/weights or special training paradigm from the training phase (model-agnostic, post-hoc); (3) handling both random-sample and whole-class forgetting with the same mechanism; and (4) proposing a set of 'practical rules' a good unlearning algorithm should satisfy. The paper claims this is the first application of the FIM diagonal to unlearning for computer-vision models. Prior FIM-based (DeltaGrad/Wu et al., FisherMask) and Hessian-based (L-CODEC, Fisher Forgetting) methods described in Related Work are cited as baselines, NOT part of this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Privacy leakage from a trained model that has memorized sensitive/private training data. The concrete adversary is a Membership Inference Attack (MIA): an attacker who queries the model to determine whether a specific record was in the training set. The regulatory driver is the 'right to be forgotten' (GDPR). The defender wants to remove a designated forget-set Df (either random samples or an entire class) so that the resulting model behaves like a 'gold' model retrained from scratch on only the retain set Dr, i.e. treats Df like never-seen test data. The paper explicitly warns against the 'Streisand effect' (driving AccDf and MIA to zero is itself a leakage signal), so the success criterion is closeness to the gold model, not maximal degradation. Note: no active poisoning/evasion adversary is assumed; the security frame is a privacy/compliance one.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) gamma is chosen qualitatively/manually, not via principled optimization; (2) unlearning quality inherently depends on the (unexamined) quality/'saturation' of the pretrained starting model, and existing metrics (MIA, AccDf) are argued to be insufficient; (3) the FIM diagonal is computed only once, before fine-tuning, rather than dynamically re-estimated during fine-tuning. Reviewer-evident from the text: evaluation is restricted to small image-classification CNNs (ResNet18/VGG-16 on MNIST/Cifar-10/Cifar-100) despite 'model-agnostic' claims - no transformers, NLP, or larger models tested; MIA is a single logistic-regression attack, so privacy guarantees are empirical, not formal (unlike differential privacy, which the paper explicitly distinguishes from unlearning); results in Tables 2-3 show DeepClean can still leave large positive Delta-MIA on Cifar-100 (e.g. VGG-16 RN Delta-MIA +23.06%), indicating incomplete forgetting on harder datasets; and code release is contingent on paper acceptance (not available).

quantitative_results: DeepClean Cifar-10 VGG-16 random-sample: AccDr 95.62%, Delta-AccDf -3.88%, Delta-MIA -2.84%, time 60s (Table 1)
DeepClean Cifar-10 ResNet-18 random-sample: AccDr 98.74%, Delta-AccDf -3.68%, Delta-MIA -5.33%, time 71s (Table 1)
Gold model Cifar-10 VGG-16 random-sample: AccDr 92.79%, AccDf 92.60%, MIA 73.32%, time 201s (Table 1)
DeepClean Cifar-10 label unlearning: Delta-AccDf +0.00% for both VGG-16/ResNet-18; Delta-MIA +3.64% (VGG-16) / -8.04% (ResNet-18) (Table 1)
Fisher Forgetting Cifar-10 random-sample: AccDr collapses to ~10%, unlearning time 3260-3743s (Table 1)
L-CODEC Cifar-10 label unlearning: Delta-AccDf up to +100.00%, Delta-MIA +65-68% (fails to forget) (Table 1)
Ablation: Zero-Weights (no retrain) Cifar-10 RN drops AccDr to 16.50% vs DeepClean 95.62% (Table 4)
Chosen thresholds: gamma=1.1 for random-sample unlearning, gamma=2 for label unlearning; gamma=1 selects ~half of trainable weights
Cifar-100 VGG-16 random-sample: DeepClean AccDr 88.69%, Delta-AccDf +3.85%, Delta-MIA +23.06% (residual leakage on harder data) (Table 2)
baselines_compared: Sparse MU (l1-sparse + linear-decaying gamma, Jia et al.)
Fisher Forgetting (Golatkar et al., Gaussian Fisher noise)
L-CODEC (Mehta et al., randomized conditionally-independent Hessians)
